meta.note: 5 distinct incident types, 11 records. Rate-limit type repeats so Hindsight consolidates a 'weakening' observation. Retain each record as an Experience Fact (use retain_text). Pre-warm the bank with this BEFORE the demo; consolidation is async.
meta.schema: Core fields per brief: { symptom, root_cause, fix, outcome, mttr_minutes, date }. Extra fields (id, service, lesson, tags, retain_text) are helpers for retain + UI.

infrastructureChanges.change: Migrated ingress from legacy NGINX gateway to Envoy. Connection handling and rate-limit/shed rules now live at Envoy; legacy gateway shed rules became no-ops.

incidents.symptom: Payments API returning 503s; p99 latency over 8s; DB active connections pinned at max (100/100).
incidents.root_cause: Connection pool exhausted - a slow analytics query held connections open with no statement timeout or backpressure.
incidents.fix: Raised pool to 200, added 5s statement_timeout, added backpressure with a circuit breaker on the analytics path.
incidents.lesson: 503s with DB connections at max means pool exhaustion - add statement_timeout and backpressure, not just a bigger pool.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-04-02 the Payments API returned 503s with p99 latency over 8s and DB connections pinned at 100/100. I diagnosed connection pool exhaustion caused by a slow analytics query holding connections with no timeout. I applied: raised the pool to 200, added a 5s statement_timeout, and added backpressure with a circuit breaker on the analytics path. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 95 minutes.

incidents.symptom: All HTTPS requests failing with SSL handshake errors across every service; clients see ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID.
incidents.root_cause: Wildcard TLS certificate expired; the auto-renewal cron had silently failed about 30 days earlier.
incidents.fix: Rotated the cert manually, restored cert-manager auto-renewal, added an expiry alert at T-14 days.
incidents.lesson: Mass handshake failures across all services means check certificate expiry first; alert before expiry, never after.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-04-18 all HTTPS requests across services failed with SSL handshake errors (ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID). I diagnosed an expired wildcard TLS cert whose auto-renewal cron had silently failed. I applied: rotated the cert, restored cert-manager auto-renewal, and added a T-14-day expiry alert. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 40 minutes.

incidents.symptom: Recommendation worker pods OOMKilled in a loop; job queue backing up; pods in CrashLoopBackOff.
incidents.root_cause: Memory leak in an unbounded in-memory embedding cache.
incidents.fix: Restarted the pods to clear memory.
incidents.lesson: Restarting an OOMKilled worker that has a leak only delays the next crash and masks the root cause. Do NOT just restart.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-04-25 the Recommendation Worker pods were OOMKilled in a loop with the queue backing up. I tried: restarting the pods to clear memory. Outcome: FAILURE - the pods OOMKilled again within minutes because the underlying memory leak remained. Lesson: do not just restart an OOMKilled worker with a leak; it masks the root cause.

incidents.symptom: Recommendation worker pods OOMKilled in a loop (recurrence); job queue backing up.
incidents.root_cause: Unbounded in-memory embedding cache growing without limit (the leak from INC-003).
incidents.fix: Bounded the cache with an LRU (max 10k entries), set container memory request/limit, fixed the leak, added a memory alert at 80%.
incidents.lesson: OOMKill in a worker - cap the cache with an LRU, set memory limits, and fix the leak. Restart is not a fix.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-04-26 the Recommendation Worker pods were OOMKilled again. I diagnosed an unbounded in-memory embedding cache leaking memory. I applied: bounded the cache with an LRU (max 10k), set container memory request/limit, fixed the leak, and added an 80% memory alert. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 110 minutes. This replaced the failed restart approach from the prior incident.

incidents.symptom: Login latency spike with cascading 429s during a traffic surge; client retries amplifying load.
incidents.root_cause: No load shedding; retry storm; cold token cache.
incidents.fix: Enabled load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed the token cache, added jittered backoff on clients.
incidents.lesson: Auth 429 cascade during a surge - shed load, warm the cache, add jittered backoff.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-04-08 the Auth Service had a login latency spike with cascading 429s during a traffic surge, amplified by client retries. I diagnosed no load shedding plus a retry storm and a cold token cache. I applied: enabled load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed the token cache, and added jittered backoff. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 60 minutes.

incidents.symptom: Login 429 cascade during a traffic surge; latency climbing.
incidents.root_cause: Surge with no shedding; cold token cache.
incidents.fix: Enabled load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed the token cache, jittered backoff.
incidents.lesson: Same shed + warm-cache + backoff playbook resolves the Auth 429 cascade.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-04-22 the Auth Service hit a login 429 cascade during a surge. I applied the known playbook: load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed token cache, jittered backoff. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 35 minutes - faster than last time.

incidents.symptom: 429 cascade on login during peak traffic.
incidents.fix: Load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed token cache, jittered backoff.
incidents.lesson: Shed + warm-cache + backoff is the reliable Auth surge playbook.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-05-05 the Auth Service hit a 429 cascade at peak traffic. I applied the established playbook: shed at the legacy gateway, warm cache, jittered backoff. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 25 minutes.

incidents.fix: Load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed token cache, jittered backoff.
incidents.lesson: Playbook is now well-proven for the Auth 429 cascade (4th success).
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-05-12 the Auth Service hit a 429 cascade during a surge. I applied the proven playbook: shed at the legacy gateway, warm cache, jittered backoff. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 20 minutes. This mitigation has now worked 4 times.

incidents.symptom: Login 429 cascade during a surge; load shedding NOT engaging like before.
incidents.root_cause: After the 2026-05-15 Envoy ingress migration, the legacy-gateway shed rule is a no-op; shedding must now be configured at Envoy.
incidents.fix: Applied the old playbook: load shedding at the legacy gateway, warmed cache, jittered backoff.
incidents.lesson: Post-Envoy-migration the legacy shed rule does nothing. The old playbook is starting to FAIL - shedding must move to Envoy.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-05-28 the Auth Service hit a 429 cascade but load shedding did not engage as it used to. I applied the old playbook (shed at the legacy gateway, warm cache, backoff). Outcome: FAILURE - shedding did not take effect. Root cause: after the 2026-05-15 Envoy ingress migration, the legacy gateway shed rule is a no-op and shedding must be configured at Envoy. The old playbook is no longer reliable.

incidents.symptom: 429 cascade on login during a surge; shedding again not engaging.
incidents.root_cause: Legacy-gateway shed rule still a no-op post-Envoy-migration.
incidents.fix: Tried the old playbook again (legacy gateway shed); also began moving shed config to Envoy.
incidents.lesson: Confirmed: the legacy shed playbook is dead post-migration. Move shedding to Envoy. This is the 2nd failure since the migration.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-06-03 the Auth Service hit another 429 cascade and the legacy shed rule again did not engage. I tried the old playbook (legacy gateway shed) - Outcome: FAILURE for the second time since the Envoy migration. Confirmed the legacy shed rule is a no-op; shedding must be configured at Envoy. The shed-at-legacy-gateway mitigation is now unreliable.

incidents.symptom: Users see stale order status immediately after placing an order; read replica lag spiked to 12s.
incidents.root_cause: Critical read-after-write was routed to a lagging read replica.
incidents.fix: Routed read-after-write and other critical reads to the primary; added a replica-lag alert at over 2s.
incidents.lesson: Stale reads right after writes - route critical reads to the primary and alert on replica lag.
incidents.retain_text: On 2026-05-20 Orders API users saw stale order status right after placing orders, with read replica lag at 12s. I diagnosed a read-after-write routed to a lagging replica. I applied: routed critical reads to the primary and added a replica-lag alert above 2s. Outcome: SUCCESS, resolved in 50 minutes.
